output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.6, "R1.2": 0.7, "R1.3": 0.8, "R1.4": 0.9, "R1.7": 0.95, "R1.6": 0.5}}

Summary: The section reads cleanly but contains a serious scholarship problem and several framing claims that need to be tightened before this can stand as an introduction/related-work passage.

Major issues

1. The framing of prior work is anachronistic and misleading.
   - Guo et al. (2017), "On Calibration of Modern Neural Networks," is real, but calling temperature scaling the "de facto current standard" in 2026 without citing any work from the intervening 9 years is implausible. A great deal has happened since: Platt scaling variants, Dirichlet calibration (Kull et al., 2019), focal-loss calibration (Mukhoti et al., 2020), ensemble/Bayesian approaches, and ASR-specific confidence work (Li et al., Kumar et al., Laptev et al., several SLT/Interspeech papers 2019–2024). The claim needs either narrowing ("remains a widely used baseline") or a much broader citation set.
   - Hannun et al. (2014) is "Deep Speech," which is not a confidence-aware ASR model and does not, to my knowledge, introduce an entropy regularizer over CTC outputs for confidence estimation. Calling it "the most recent confidence-aware ASR model" is doubly wrong: (a) it is 12 years old as of the stated 2026 date, and (b) the paper's contribution is end-to-end ASR, not confidence calibration. This citation appears to be fabricated or badly misattributed and must be replaced. Plausible real comparators include Kumar et al. (2020) on confidence estimation for end-to-end ASR, Li et al. (2021) on word-level confidence for RNN-T, or Laptev & Ginsburg (2023).
   - Park et al. (2025) and Yamamoto et al. (2025) on "calibrated tool-use agents" should be verified — I cannot confirm these exist as cited, and the introduction leans on them to motivate novelty.

2. The novelty claim is overstated.
   - "First systematic study of token-level recalibration in modern transformer ASR encoders" is a strong claim that the cited landscape does not support. Token- and word-level confidence in transformer/RNN-T ASR has been studied repeatedly (Google's word-confidence work for RNN-T, NVIDIA's NeMo confidence module, etc.). Either soften ("first to apply X to Y under Z conditions") or add a paragraph distinguishing the contribution from those lines.

3. Evidence vs. claim gaps.
   - The numbers (ECE 3.1 vs 5.8 on test-clean; 7.4 → 4.2 on test-other) are presented without indicating: n bins used for ECE, temperature value learned, validation/test split discipline, or whether the baseline was re-implemented or quoted. Reviewers will ask. Also, "WER regression below 0.1 absolute" — state the actual WER numbers, since calibration that touches the decoder is the main risk.
   - "Per-token reliability diagrams" referenced as Figure 3 — token-level reliability diagrams require a definition of the per-token correctness target (substitution vs deletion handling under alignment). Add a sentence or footnote.
   - The high-confidence-bin improvement claim is the most interesting result; it deserves a number, not just a figure pointer.

4. Logical chain around the motivation.
   - The opening jumps from "voice assistants/dialogue/tool-use agents" to LibriSpeech read speech without acknowledging the domain gap. Tool-use routing decisions live on conversational/far-field audio; LibriSpeech results are weak evidence for the motivating application. Either widen the eval (e.g., add a noisy or conversational set) or scope the motivation more honestly.

Smaller writing notes

- "posteriors that match empirical correctness" — fine, but the next sentence reduces this to a frequentist calibration statement; pick one framing and stick to it.
- "without altering the argmax decoding" is true for global temperature scaling but not generally true for a "token-level recalibration head," which is what you actually propose. Clarify that your head preserves argmax (if it does) or acknowledge the WER risk explicitly.
- "spoken tool-use agents that act on transcribed commands" is a good hook — consider returning to it in the results paragraph to close the loop.
- "the most recent confidence-aware ASR model of Hannun et al. (2014)" — "most recent" and "2014" cannot coexist; this phrasing alone will trigger a desk-reject reflex.

Recommended priority order: (1) fix the Hannun et al. citation and verify Park/Yamamoto; (2) broaden the post-2017 calibration citations; (3) soften or sharpen the novelty claim; (4) tighten the empirical reporting; (5) address the LibriSpeech-vs-tool-use domain gap.

Uncertainty disclosure: I have not run a literature search to confirm specific replacement citations (Kumar 2020, Li 2021, Laptev & Ginsburg 2023, Kull 2019, Mukhoti 2020) — they are offered as starting points and authors/years should be verified before use. I cannot confirm or deny the existence of Park et al. (2025) and Yamamoto et al. (2025) without a search.
